We want to find the least number of children we can choose to guarantee that we have pencils of all colors.

We have $c = 4$ colors and $p = 6$ pencils of each color.
We are giving $6$ children $4$ pencils each, so we have $6 \times 4 = 24$ pencils in total.
Since there are $6$ colors and $6$ pencils of each color, we have $6 \times 6 = 36$ pencils.
We have $24$ pencils and $6$ children.
We want to find the smallest $k$ such that we are guaranteed to have pencils of all colors.
We can use the worst-case scenario. We can choose $k$ children such that we have as few colors as possible.
We can choose $k$ children such that we have at most $c-1 = 3$ colors.
If we choose $k = 4$, we can have $k$ children such that we have at most $3$ colors.
We want to find the smallest $k$ such that we are guaranteed to have at least one pencil of each color.
We can use the formula $k = c + (c-1) = 4 + 3 = 7$.
So we need to choose $k = 7$ children.
